result: I keep thinking about the way light pools on my kitchen counter at 3:17 p.m., how it arrives without invitation, stays for exactly forty-three minutes, then slips away like the cat who pretends ownership of the doorway. That daily visitation feels more trustworthy than the headlines that metastasize every hour, each one screaming newest, biggest, unprecedented until language itself seems bruised. Between those bulletins I watch the rosemary cutting I stuck in a jelly jar—it has no business rooting, but it does, pale filaments reaching into tap water we pretend is sterile. Entire civilizations of bacteria must be cheering inside, holding microscopic elections, protesting whatever counts as gentrification when you’re only one cell wide. I like imagining their debates more than ours: no one tweets about the ethics of dividing near the meniscus, they just split and keep photosynthesizing. Meanwhile my neighbor drags her trash bins past while AirPods seal her off; we wave like strangers who once shared sugar during a blackout, mouths remembering emergency kindness. Somewhere a satellite corrects its own orbit, nudging itself away from collision with the clutter we’ve thrown up there—old phones, rocket gloves, a spatula that floated out of a space-station hatch. If aliens arrive they’ll think the sky is our landfill, earth the tidy bedroom we shove everything under. Yet that rogue rosemary keeps issuing tiny green holographs of future flavor, insisting possibility is a weed, not a gift certificate. I set my watch by the light puddle, refuse to check the news until the sun moves on; forty-three minutes of quiet is revolution enough.

Between those bulletins I watch the rosemary cutting I stuck in a jelly jar—it has no business rooting, but it does, pale filaments reaching into tap water we pretend is sterile. Entire civilizations of bacteria must be cheering inside, holding microscopic elections, protesting whatever counts as gentrification when you’re only one cell wide. I like imagining their debates more than ours: no one tweets about the ethics of dividing near the meniscus, they just split and keep photosynthesizing. Meanwhile my neighbor drags her trash bins past while AirPods seal her off; we wave like strangers who once shared sugar during a blackout, mouths remembering emergency kindness. Somewhere a satellite corrects its own orbit, nudging itself away from collision with the clutter we’ve thrown up there—old phones, rocket gloves, a spatula that floated out of a space-station hatch. If aliens arrive they’ll think the sky is our landfill, earth the tidy bedroom we shove everything under. Yet that rogue rosemary keeps issuing tiny green holographs of future flavor, insisting possibility is a weed, not a gift certificate.
